cataloging_subject.label: A Noble Boke off Cookry
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-medieval English culinary compilation represented by CME00005. It is distinct from the physical Holkham Hall manuscript MS 674, Robina Napier's 1882 edition and its modern paratext, the University of Michigan digital encoding, and the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: A Noble Boke off Cookry
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous compiler(s)
catalog_summary.date: Compiled after 1467 according to Napier; surviving manuscript dated c. 1480-1500 by the Fitzwilliam Museum
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of compilation and dialect region unresolved

notes: This concise established title is derived from the manuscript's closing and Napier's edition title; no separate medieval title page is known.

titles.value: A Noble Boke off Cookry ffor a Prynce Houssolde or Eny Other Estately Houssolde

assertion.notes: Napier uses this wording on the 1882 edition title page; spelling and capitalization vary in modern catalogs.

agents.name: Robina Napier
notes: Napier edited and transcribed the 1882 source edition. She is not the author or compiler of the medieval work.

assertion.notes: The compilation is anonymous, and its menu and recipe materials may have been assembled from more than one older source.

scope.description: The 1882 printed edition through which CME00005 transmits the compilation
Napier's main transcription of Holkham Hall MS 674 on printed pages 1-123

assertion.notes: Napier dates the written compilation after the 1467 Neville feast and probably shortly thereafter; the Fitzwilliam Museum dates the surviving manuscript c. 1480-1500. This range does not date every underlying recipe.

notes: Do not normalize Napier's relative argument to a single year such as 1468.

places.label: England
notes: The surviving manuscript is assigned to England by the Fitzwilliam Museum. The exact place where the compilation was made remains unresolved.
Do not infer a place of compilation from the manuscript's present location at Holkham Hall or from the 1882 edition's London imprint.

scope.description: French headings and mottos embedded in the feast menus

form_statements.description: Recipe directions, feast menus, service lists, and the calendar are transmitted as prose or prose lists. Brief rhyming phrases and mottos do not make the compilation mixed-form.

subject_statements.label: Household food service and course organization

summaries.value: An anonymous late-fifteenth-century English culinary compilation preserved in Holkham Hall MS 674. It opens with historical feast menus and household service fragments, supplies a calendar of dishes, and transmits 253 extant recipe units in the U-M encoding. Napier reports that 27 recipes once expected by the calendar are missing from the surviving manuscript.

content_parts.label: Collection of feast menus
content_parts.summary: Ten historical royal and aristocratic feast-menu units, many divided into courses, including the 1467 installation feast of George Neville as Archbishop of York.
notes: The ten top-level menu units contain twelve nested course DIV3 elements and 337 XML ITEM elements; ITEM is a markup count, not a count of separate works.

content_parts.label: Monthly household-service fragments
content_parts.summary: January and February household services followed by a fragmentary first course.

content_parts.label: Calendar of the book of cookery
content_parts.summary: An alphabetical-style calendar or finding list of dishes intended to precede the recipe text.
notes: The item_count is the number of XML ITEM nodes, not a canonical count of recipes. Napier reports 27 calendared recipes missing from the surviving manuscript, while the current XML encodes 253 extant recipe DIV2 units.

content_parts.label: Collection of recipes
content_parts.summary: Two hundred fifty-three extant culinary recipe units covering pottages, sauces, meat, poultry, fish, pastries, sweets, and dishes for ordinary, feast, and fasting service.
notes: The XML closes with the manuscript trailer beginning 'Her endithe a noble book of cookry'.

extent.description: The central recipe collection contains 253 encoded DIV2 recipe units. The complete XML representation also contains ten top-level feast-menu DIV2 units, twelve nested course DIV3 units, three monthly-service or fragment DIV2 units, 274 calendar ITEM nodes, and 124 PB milestones. Napier states that 27 recipes represented in the calendar are missing from the surviving manuscript. These are representation and transmission counts rather than a claim that the abstract compilation originally had exactly 280 uniformly bounded recipes.

related_works.title: The Forme of Cury
notes: Napier compares recipes by name and substance. This is a relationship for discovery, not proof that the Holkham compilation copied directly from a surviving Forme of Cury witness.

open_questions.question: Can the place of compilation and dialect region be established more narrowly than England?
next_steps: Consult a modern linguistic or codicological study based directly on Holkham Hall MS 674.
Keep the work's compilation context distinct from the manuscript's present Holkham location and Napier's London publication place.

open_questions.question: How many recipes did the compilation contain before the losses identified by Napier?
next_steps: Collate the manuscript calendar, surviving recipe divisions, and any lost-leaf evidence from a complete manuscript facsimile.
Do not add the 274 XML calendar items mechanically to, or equate them with, the 253 encoded recipe units.

evidence.citation: General Corpus / CME source XML, CME00005.xml.
evidence.summary: Identifies CME00005 and its 1882 source edition; encodes title matter, feast menus, monthly-service fragments, a calendar, 253 recipe divisions, and the final manuscript trailer, with a 2016 revision note.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse, item CME00005.
evidence.locator: item information, table of contents, full text, and rights statement
evidence.summary: The official item identifies Napier's 1882 edition, exposes the encoded menus and recipe headings, supplies cooking subject terms, and applies the No Copyright-United States statement.

evidence.citation: A Noble Boke off Cookry ffor a Prynce Houssolde or Eny Other Estately Houssolde: Reprinted Verbatim from a Rare MS. in the Holkham Collection, ed. Mrs. Alexander Napier (London: Elliot Stock, 1882).
evidence.locator: title page; introduction pp. v-xiii; medieval transcription pp. 1-123; note after p. 123; appendix and glossary pp. 124-136
evidence.summary: The immediate printed source names Napier and Holkham, argues for compilation after the 1467 Neville feast, claims a verbatim transcription, prints the medieval menus, calendar, recipes, and trailer, and reports 27 recipes missing between 'Zoupes chett' and 'Lesk lombard'.

evidence.citation: The Fitzwilliam Museum, Feast & Fast exhibition label, 'Cookery'.
evidence.summary: The institutional label identifies an anonymous manuscript made in England c. 1480-1500, kept at Holkham Hall as MS 674, written in Middle English, and containing feast menus followed by more than 280 recipes.

evidence.citation: R. N. [Robina Napier], 'An Early Cookery Book,' The Antiquary 4 (September 1881): 96-98.
evidence.locator: printed pp. 96-98; scanned PDF pages 8-10; viewer leaves n7-n9
evidence.summary: Napier's contemporary description characterizes the manuscript as a small rebound volume of about eighty leaves, with black text and red headings or ornament, some damp damage, and feast menus on the first eleven leaves.

Counts 10 feast-menu DIV2 units, 12 nested course DIV3 units, 3 monthly-service or fragment DIV2 units, 274 calendar ITEM nodes, 253 recipe DIV2 units, 280 HEAD elements, 284 P elements, and 124 PB milestones.

notes: The Fitzwilliam label's phrase 'over 280 recipes' is retained as its institutional description but is not used as an exact item count because Napier and the XML expose different calendar, surviving-text, and loss counts.
The medieval textual content and physical manuscript are modeled separately from Napier's editorial work and the U-M encoding.
